summary: This brief technical post documents a workaround for the .NET WebClient class's lack of native HTTP compression support. Atwood shows how to add gzip/deflate headers to requests and then decompress the response using the third-party SharpZipLib library. The post serves as a quick how-to for developers needing to reduce bandwidth usage in their .NET applications. It's a straightforward code snippet post typical of early technical blogging.
key_points: Net.WebClient doesn't natively support HTTP compression despite being able to request it
Google specifically requires a User-Agent header before it will gzip responses
SharpZipLib provides an easy workaround for decompressing gzipped responses
Network sniffers are useful for verifying compression is actually working
money_quotes: The Net.WebClient class doesn't support HTTP compression
And voila, the bandwidth, you have saved eet!
key_insight: When framework classes lack features you need, third-party libraries can fill the gap with minimal additional code.
